title: Open Workshop: Designer-Builder Integration, Budget Presentation, and Women in Construction
summary: Participants discussed the challenges of integrating design services into the building process across different markets, particularly for smaller markets where designer education is still needed. The session also covered how to handle post-estimate conversations with clients, managing procurement systems, and the unique challenges women face in the construction industry. Erin shared insights from working with Studio McGee and announced upcoming guest speakers and platform resources.

teachings.concept: Knowing What You Are and Are Not as a Builder
teachings.explanation: Erin emphasizes the importance of clearly defining your role and expertise. Builders should communicate their lane — building expertise — and require the right team structure (architect, designer, builder) before taking on a project. Accepting projects outside that structure leads to problems for everyone.
teachings.quote: I know what I'm not, but if you want to go down the expertise of building, I'm your girl. Like if that's what you want.

teachings.explanation: Rather than requiring all design decisions before construction begins, effective builder-designer collaboration sets specific checkpoints tied to construction phases (foundation, framing, MEP, drywall, etc.). Each phase has its own urgent design requirements, and the builder's skill is distinguishing what is urgent versus what can be developed later.
teachings.quote: We need to hold each other accountable... if someone doesn't tell me where the sconces are going to be placed, I'm going to have to mention in a meeting that it's going to cost money to move the sconce.

teachings.explanation: No build process is ever fully perfect, and successful builders continuously improve their systems after identifying failures. Holding regular team meetings to identify and fix process gaps — such as procurement failures — is more valuable than expecting perfection from the start.
teachings.quote: I make so many mistakes during the day. I just don't tell you. But it's just part of the deal... this is one of the hardest businesses to be in. It's just the way it works. As long as you're making progress.

teachings.concept: Distinguishing Urgent vs. Non-Urgent Design Decisions
teachings.explanation: A core builder skill is filtering which design elements must be resolved at each construction phase versus which can wait. This prevents costly mistakes and keeps projects moving without requiring all decisions upfront, which is often unrealistic.
teachings.quote: All we really need to know is what firebox are we buying and what brick are we putting inside? Because I want to be prepared for the mantle later on when I measure it. And that's the skill of a great builder.

teachings.explanation: Immediately after presenting a construction estimate, Erin asks clients what they are worried about rather than waiting for them to process on their own. This opens dialogue, surfaces concerns in real time, and allows the builder to address scope and budget adjustments collaboratively before the client disengages.
teachings.quote: I look up there. I said, what are you worried about? Or are there like, what are you concerned about? Because I know this is a big number... and the floodgates open.

teachings.explanation: Experienced builders develop pattern recognition when reviewing estimates — noticing when specific line items like electrical or plumbing labor deviate from norms. Keeping a short list of five or six known areas for cost reduction allows builders to respond quickly when clients push back on budget.
teachings.quote: I always look for pattern recognition... one of the patterns is the electric labor and the plumbing labor. If you're not using a lighting design system, they hover around the same price.

teachings.explanation: Erin's approach to disrespectful or non-performing subcontractors is to enforce consistent standards of respect and quality for everyone on the job site, regardless of the cost or disruption of replacing them. Short-term pain of finding replacements is preferable to compromising team culture and standards.
teachings.quote: There are standards that I have and they learn really quickly. Like this is the standard and if that doesn't work for you, you're gone. And I've always been like that from day one.

teachings.concept: Knowledge Sharing Accelerates the Whole Industry
teachings.explanation: Builders traditionally guard their methods as competitive secrets, but sharing techniques — such as waterproofing methods or procurement systems — benefits everyone in the community and raises the quality of work across the industry. The group construction log in the program platform was created for this purpose.
teachings.quote: Normally in this, especially with builders, builders don't typically share their secrets. And so that drives me nuts. And I think we should all share with each other.

actionable_advice: Create a clear list of five required items you need from designers and architects before construction begins, and present it to your design team at the start of every project.
Immediately after presenting an estimate, ask clients 'What are you worried about?' to open dialogue and surface concerns before they disengage and try to process on their own.
Hold regular team meetings (such as weekly) with a standing agenda item to identify and improve one system or process that broke down during the previous period.
When budgets come in high, go back to the five or six pattern-recognized line items you flagged during budget-building and use those as your starting points for value engineering conversations.
Follow up every major estimate meeting with an email that summarizes concerns discussed, next steps, and who owns each action item.
Post two stories per day on social media showing who you are and how you operate — use job site walkthroughs and construction details as content.
Upload videos to the group construction log platform to share building techniques and methods with other program members.

Study how men communicate in professional contexts — particularly the use of side-by-side positioning rather than face-to-face confrontation — to improve effectiveness when managing subcontractor relationships.

Begin educating clients early in smaller markets where designers are not yet seen as essential, using consistent language about why the architect-designer-builder team structure protects their investment.
Consider connecting with the Professional Women in Building group through NAHB if you are a woman in construction seeking community and professional development.
client_challenges: In smaller markets like Huntsville, Alabama, clients are not accustomed to hiring designers early in the process and resist the additional time and cost of thorough pre-construction design work.

Clients experience sticker shock when build estimates come in higher than expected, and they disengage to 'figure it out' without keeping the builder in the loop, creating scheduling and commitment uncertainty.
Designers and builder-designers struggle with how to structure hybrid models where design work continues through the build rather than being fully completed before construction starts.
Procurement systems break down during team transitions — such as a project manager going on maternity leave — leading to missed orders and unexpected out-of-pocket costs for the builder.
Women in construction face unconscious bias from subcontractors who are not accustomed to taking direction from female builders, sometimes resulting in disrespect or insubordination on job sites.
Clients often want a fast, 'Amazon-style' design and build process and resist the intentional, slow process required for high-quality custom projects.
Builders and designers working together struggle to know exactly how much allowance to assign for selections when design is not complete, risking budgets that are significantly off.
notable_quotes: On walking away from bad-fit clients: 'That's not the product we deliver. I'm out. But that's how much you have to pay attention to these things when you're communicating with people.'
On adaptability: 'Adaptable is great in the spirit of progress. Adaptable is bad when you're responding to what you know isn't right.'

On women in construction: 'I think it's frankly made me a much better builder because I learned I'm not going to open my mouth unless I've studied this... when I am going to say something, I'm going to back it up.'

On the reality of design during construction: 'I will tell you firsthand, it was developed during construction. Now, it did get developed with guidelines and we had conversations — by frame, by foundation, we need this.'

On managing large project teams: 'The more people there are, the faster you have to filter and the faster you have to let them know — don't give me any answers that don't have anything to do with my question.'
On sharing industry knowledge: 'In the days of fax machines... it was learning from others. So I'm just a huge proponent of people sharing their knowledge.'
